title: Unit 11 Checkpoint Quiz — Regional Language & Slang

questions.prompt: What is 'mande' and why is it culturally important?
options: A uniquely Mexican polite way to say 'pardon?' — more respectful than '¿qué?'
A way to call a waiter
questions.explanation: 'Mande' (from mandar = to command) is uniquely Mexican. Using it instead of '¿Qué?' to say 'pardon?' is considered more polite and respectful. It immediately signals cultural awareness.
'Chilango/a' = a person from Mexico City (CDMX). The term can be used neutrally or with affectionate teasing depending on context. CDMX residents may also call themselves chilangos proudly.

questions.prompt: How are diminutives (-ito/-ita) used in Mexican Spanish?
options: Much more than in other Spanish-speaking countries — to show affection and warmth, not just smallness
The same way as in all other Spanish-speaking countries
questions.explanation: Mexicans use diminutives far more than any other Spanish-speaking country. 'Cafecito' isn't necessarily a small coffee — it's a warm, friendly way to ask for coffee. Diminutives soften requests and show affection.

questions.prompt: If someone says 'ese producto es muy chafa,' what do they mean?
options: That product is really low quality / junk.
questions.explanation: 'Chafa' = low quality, poorly made, shoddy. It's used nationwide in Mexico to describe things that are cheap in the bad sense — not just inexpensive, but poorly made.
'Está cañón' is widely used Mexican slang meaning something is tough, intense, or crazy. 'El tráfico está cañón' = traffic is brutal. 'El examen estuvo cañón' = the exam was rough.

options: An outsider / someone from outside the local area
questions.explanation: 'Fuereño/a' = outsider / from out of town. It can be warm or teasing: 'Se te nota que eres fuereño' = You can tell you're not from here. Not necessarily a foreigner — just someone from another region.

questions.prompt: What does 'a poco' express in CDMX slang?
options: Really? / No way! — surprise or disbelief
questions.explanation: 'A poco' in Mexico City = Really? / No way! '— Me casé. — ¡A poco!' = 'I got married. — No way!' It expresses genuine surprise. Don't confuse with 'un poco' (a little bit).

questions.prompt: Which of these is associated with Guadalajara / Jalisco (tapatío culture)?
questions.explanation: Mariachi music and tequila are both from Jalisco — the heartland of tapatío culture. Guadalajara is also the birthplace of the Mexican Hat Dance (El Jarabe Tapatío). Mole negro is from Oaxaca; henequén is from Yucatán.
